questions.q: By what other well-known name is the sacred city of Kashi commonly called today?
questions.explanation: Kashi is the ancient name of the city now widely known as Varanasi (Banaras).

questions.q: On the banks of which sacred river does the holy city of Kashi stand?
questions.explanation: Kashi stands on the banks of the river Ganga, regarded as India's most sacred river.

questions.q: In the Puranas, Kashi is hailed as the earthly city of which great deity?
questions.explanation: The Puranas extol Kashi as the beloved earthly city of Lord Shiva.

questions.q: The name Kashi is traditionally linked to a Sanskrit root meaning 'to shine,' giving the city which famous poetic epithet?
questions.explanation: The name Kashi is associated with a root meaning 'to shine,' which is why the city is celebrated as the 'City of Light.'

questions.q: By what name is the presiding form of Shiva worshipped at Kashi best known?
questions.explanation: The presiding deity of Kashi is Shiva in his form as Vishwanath, Lord of the Universe.

questions.q: The name 'Vishwanath,' the presiding form of Shiva at Kashi, is traditionally understood to mean what?
questions.explanation: Vishwanath means 'Lord of the Universe,' the title under which Shiva is revered at Kashi.

questions.q: According to Puranic tradition, what blessing is said to await those who die within the sacred bounds of Kashi?
questions.explanation: Tradition holds that those who die in Kashi are freed from the cycle of rebirth.

questions.q: Kashi is counted among which category of revered locations in Hindu tradition?
options: The royal capitals of the Mahabharata
The holiest sacred cities of India
questions.explanation: Kashi is counted among the holiest of India's sacred cities (tirthas).

questions.q: The trident emblem associated with Kashi is the characteristic weapon of which deity?
questions.explanation: The trident (trishula) is the iconic weapon of Shiva, the presiding lord of Kashi.

questions.q: Kashi is regarded in Hindu tradition primarily as which kind of place?
questions.explanation: Kashi is revered above all as a sacred pilgrimage city of immense spiritual significance.

questions.q: According to Puranic lore, what is unique about Kashi during the great cosmic dissolution (pralaya)?
options: It is the first city created anew
It is submerged beneath the ocean
It is never abandoned or destroyed
It is carried into the heavens
questions.explanation: The Puranas hold that Kashi is never abandoned, remaining intact even during cosmic dissolution.

questions.q: Tradition holds that the dying in Kashi receive a liberating mantra whispered by which deity?
questions.explanation: It is said that Shiva himself whispers his liberating mantra (the Taraka mantra) to those who die in Kashi.

questions.q: The famous temple at the heart of Kashi is dedicated to Shiva under which name?
questions.explanation: The principal temple of the city is the Kashi Vishwanath temple, dedicated to Shiva as Vishwanath.

questions.q: The Shiva shrine at Kashi is traditionally counted among which renowned group of twelve sacred sites?
questions.explanation: Kashi Vishwanath is revered as one of the twelve Jyotirlingas, sacred manifestations of Shiva as a pillar of light.

questions.q: Kashi is traditionally included among the seven sacred cities said to grant liberation, known by what collective term?
questions.explanation: Kashi is one of the Sapta Puri, the seven holy cities believed to bestow liberation (moksha).

questions.q: The fierce guardian form of Shiva who is venerated as the protector of Kashi is known by which name?
questions.explanation: Kala Bhairava is honored as the fierce guardian and protector of the sacred city of Kashi.

questions.q: The riverside steps in Kashi where pilgrims bathe and perform rituals along the Ganga are called what?
questions.explanation: The stepped embankments along the Ganga at Kashi are known as ghats, central to its pilgrimage life.

questions.q: In Puranic tradition, the descent of the Ganga to earth was made possible when its fall was broken by what?
questions.explanation: The Puranas recount that the Ganga's mighty descent was broken by Shiva, who caught her in his matted locks.

questions.q: Which divine consort is traditionally worshipped alongside Shiva Vishwanath at Kashi?
questions.explanation: At Kashi the goddess Annapurna, a form of Parvati, is revered as the consort of Shiva Vishwanath.

questions.q: The goddess Annapurna, venerated at Kashi as Shiva's consort, is especially associated with the gift of what?
questions.explanation: Annapurna is revered as the goddess of food and nourishment, said to feed all who come to Kashi.

questions.q: Kashi is described in tradition as one of the most ancient continuously inhabited places in the world. Which faith, alongside Hinduism, also reveres sites in the Kashi region?
questions.explanation: Sarnath near Kashi is a major Buddhist site, where the Buddha is said to have given his first sermon.

questions.q: In Hindu tradition, the act of dying and being liberated at Kashi is often referred to by which term?
questions.explanation: The liberation believed to be attained by dying in Kashi is traditionally called Kashi-labha, 'the gain of Kashi.'

questions.q: The Skanda Purana contains an extensive section glorifying the sacred city of Kashi. By what name is this section known?
questions.explanation: The Kashi Khanda of the Skanda Purana is the major Puranic text devoted to glorifying Kashi.

questions.q: A river that joins the Ganga at Kashi gives the city its modern name Varanasi. Which two rivers are traditionally said to bound the sacred city?
questions.explanation: The name Varanasi is traditionally derived from the rivers Varuna and Assi, which bound the sacred city.

questions.q: In the Mahabharata, the kingdom of Kashi was ruled by a king whose three daughters were carried off by Bhishma for his brother. Who was this king?
options: The King of Kashi (father of Amba, Ambika and Ambalika)
questions.explanation: In the Mahabharata, Bhishma carried off Amba, Ambika and Ambalika, the three princesses of Kashi, for Vichitravirya.

questions.q: Puranic lore associates the cremation ground at Kashi with Shiva's role in death and liberation. By what name is this famed burning ghat known?
questions.explanation: Manikarnika is the celebrated cremation ghat at Kashi, deeply tied to its sanctity as a place of liberation in death.

questions.q: According to one Puranic tradition, the city of Kashi rests not upon the earth but is upheld upon which of Shiva's emblems?
questions.explanation: Puranic lore holds that Kashi rests upon the tip of Shiva's trident, set apart from the ordinary earth.

questions.q: In the Puranas, a king named Divodasa once ruled Kashi so righteously that Shiva had to use stratagems to reclaim the city. What was the nature of Divodasa's rule that posed this difficulty?
options: He had grown immortal through a boon
He had hidden the city beneath the sea
His rule was so perfectly just that no fault could be found
questions.explanation: Puranic tradition tells that King Divodasa ruled Kashi so flawlessly that Shiva could find no pretext to displace him and reclaim his city.

questions.q: Kashi is one of the Sapta Puri liberation-cities. Which of the following is also traditionally counted among these seven sacred cities?
questions.explanation: Ayodhya is, like Kashi, one of the seven Sapta Puri cities believed to grant liberation.

questions.q: In several Puranas Kashi is given an honorific epithet that, like its 'City of Light' name, derives from a Sanskrit root for 'to shine.' Which Sanskrit name reflects this meaning?
questions.explanation: The name Kashi itself derives from a Sanskrit root meaning 'to shine,' yielding the sense of the luminous or shining city.
